text: The camera provides a view from the suprapubic port, showing the surgical field where the surgeon examines the fibrous stenotic disease.
The camera view from the suprapubic port reveals visible strictures in the colon, indicative of fibrous stenotic disease.
The surgeon begins the procedure by tenting the iliocolic pedicle, initiating a medial to lateral dissection to separate the pedicle from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses two visible instruments to perform the procedure through a suprapubic port site.

The surgeon prepares for an intracorporeal anastomosis due to limited mobilization of the hepatic flexure, preventing the colon from reaching the suprapubic port site for extraction and extracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon visually identifies and displays the ureter, highlighting its location and orientation within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial and lateral dissection of the bowel, assessing its reach to the suprapubic port site for potential extracorporeal hand-sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon isolates the iliocolic pedicle, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon sweeps the duodenum downward and prepares to divide it using a ligature device.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection beneath the mesentery, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins a lateral approach to the dissection, shifting focus from the previously isolated iliocolic pedicle.
The surgeon pushes up on the iliocolic angle, manipulating the tissue in this region during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the ascending colon, progressing towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon ensures the mobilization of the small bowel mesentery, checking its separation from surrounding tissues.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field as the surgeon prepares to exteriorize the bowel through the Pfannenstiel or suprapubic incision, taking care to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon mobilizes tissue underneath the iliocolic pedicle, connecting it with the ongoing medial dissection.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, positioned adjacent to the dissected area.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection over the right kidney, ensuring the surrounding tissue is carefully separated.
The psoas muscle is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the bowel to ensure it reaches the site for extracorporeal anastomosis, adjusting the tissue to facilitate the upcoming surgical connection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the proximal colon mesentery, ensuring preservation of the right colic branches during the procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the hepatic flexure, detaching it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon mobilizes additional portions of the duodenum to prevent thermal spread when maneuvering over the top of the colon.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the hepatic flexure, continuing the mobilization of the colon for the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to join the medial plane, which is visibly close, approximately a cell layer away, during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of approach to dissect from the lateral side, moving back around the colon in the direction of the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon separates the colon from the duodenum, visibly creating a distinct plane between these two anatomical structures.
The surgeon examines the reach of the instruments and decides to further mobilize the surrounding tissue for better access.
The surgeon begins to exteriorize the specimen, preparing to remove the separated section of the colon from the surgical field.
The surgeon staples the proximal and distal ends of the colon and performs a hand-sewn side-to-side anastomosis.
A two and a half centimeter incision is made in the suprapubic region, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon successfully exteriorizes the small bowel, bringing it out through the incision.
The surgeon observes strictured areas within the exposed section of the bowel, indicating regions of narrowed tissue.
The surgeon inspects the newly created anastomosis, checking for any signs of tension or bleeding at the surgical site.
The surgeon repositions the anastomosis into the right quadrant of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the mesentery for any signs of twisting, confirming that no twisting has occurred.
The surgeon brings omentum from the redundant transverse colon to cover the anastomosis site, ensuring it is adequately protected.
